headline: Custer Battlefield National Monument Established, 1946
body: The wind, a dry whisper across the Montana plains, carried the scent of dust and distant thunder. It was March 22, 1946, when the Custer Battlefield National Monument was formally etched into the nation's memory, a place consecrated to a brutal truth. But the story, the real story, began long before, under a searing June sun in 1876, when the Little Bighorn River flowed red with the blood of men. It was a clash, not merely of armies, but of worlds. The Lakota, the Cheyenne, the Arapaho, their lives woven into the vast, untamed tapestry of the plains, faced the relentless advance of a nation hungry for land, for gold, for dominion. The Treaty of Fort Laramie, a fragile parchment promise, had been torn asunder by the glint of yellow metal in the Black Hills. An ultimatum, cold and unyielding, had been issued: report to the reservations or be branded
location: Little Bighorn Battlefield National Monument, Big Horn County
